coredata.dc:title: Tristable switching of the electrical conductivity through graphene quantum dots sandwiched in multi-stacked poly(methyl methacrylate) layers
coredata.dc:creator: Ooi, Poh Choon
Lin, Jian
Kim, Tae Whan
Li, Fushan
coredata.dc:description: Tristable switching nonvolatile memory (NVM) devices based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) sandwiched between multi-stacked poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) layers were fabricated on indium-tin-oxide (ITO)-coated glass substrates by using a solution-processed method. Current-voltage (I-V) curves at 300 K for the silver nanowire/PMMA/GQD/PMMA/GQD/PMMA/ITO/glass devices showed tristable switching currents with high-resistance, intermediate-resistance, and low-resistance states. The device's cycling endurance of the three resistance states remained stable with a distinguishable value for each resistance state over 1000 cycles, and the obtained retention results showed well-distinguished resistance states without degradation for up to 1 × 104 s. Schottky emission, Poole-Frenkel emission, trapped-charge limited-current, and ohmic conduction were proposed as the dominant conduction mechanisms for the fabricated NVM devices based on the obtained I-V characteristics. The described energy-band diagrams confirm the proposed conduction band mechanisms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tristable switching
Graphene quantum dot
Poly(methyl methacrylate)
Electrical characteristic
Filament
Conduction mechanisms

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank Prof. Haibo Xu from the Ocean University of China for providing the graphene quantum dots. This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the gs1:National Research Foundation of Korea funded by the gs2:Ministry of Education, Science and Technology ( 2016R1A2A1A05005502 ) and by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 61377027 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cross-sectional structures of the MIM NVM devices: (a) MIM-A without graphene quantum dots and (b) MIM-B with graphene quantum dots. The inset presents an SEM image of the cross-sectional structure for the device.
Optical transparency of the MIM-A and the MIM-B devices in the visible region.

(a) Semi-log plot of the tristable switching I–V characteristics for the MIM-B NVM device. The inset presents the I–V characteristics of the reference device, MIM-A. Electrical conductivity fittings for the (b) ON-1 state and the (c) ON state on a double log plot.

Schematic energy-band diagrams to propose the possible dominant conduction mechanisms during the Set process for the MIM-B NVM device under various applied voltages: (a) the initial state at zero bias, (b) from the OFF state to the intermediate V
ON1 state, (c) the intermediate ON-1 state and (d) from the intermediate ON-1 state to the ON state.
Data from the (a) endurance test and (b) retention stability test of the MIM-B NVM devices.

head.ce:title: Tristable switching of the electrical conductivity through graphene quantum dots sandwiched in multi-stacked poly(methyl methacrylate) layers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronics and Computer Engineering, Hanyang University, Seoul 133-791, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electronics and Computer Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Optoelectronic Display, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350002, People's Republic of China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: P.C. Ooi and J. Lin contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tristable switching nonvolatile memory (NVM) devices based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) sandwiched between multi-stacked poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) layers were fabricated on indium-tin-oxide (ITO)-coated glass substrates by using a solution-processed method. Current-voltage (I-V) curves at 300 K for the silver nanowire/PMMA/GQD/PMMA/GQD/PMMA/ITO/glass devices showed tristable switching currents with high-resistance, intermediate-resistance, and low-resistance states. The device's cycling endurance of the three resistance states remained stable with a distinguishable value for each resistance state over 1000 cycles, and the obtained retention results showed well-distinguished resistance states without degradation for up to 1 × 104 s. Schottky emission, Poole-Frenkel emission, trapped-charge limited-current, and ohmic conduction were proposed as the dominant conduction mechanisms for the fabricated NVM devices based on the obtained I-V characteristics. The described energy-band diagrams confirm the proposed conduction band mechanisms.

ce:section.ce:para: The development in research on multilevel switching nonvolatile memories (NVMs) currently takes place due to the demand for higher density data storage. Multilevel memories offer the unique advantage of being able to store more than 2 bits of data in a single device, thus significantly enhancing the data storage with minimal down scaling [1–6]. The NVM devices reported to date have been designed in a metal-insulator-metal (MIM) structure for resistance switching applications owing to their simple two-terminal structure, low cost, excellent memory characteristics and great scale-down potential [7–9]. As reported elsewhere, several metal oxides, organic materials, semiconductor quantum dots, and carbon-based materials have shown potential for use as charge-trapping media [10–15].

Among the various kinds of charge trapping materials, carbon-based materials, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), are explored as alternatives to the materials as mentioned above for use in NVM memory devices because of their superior properties, for instance, superior chemical inertness, low toxicity, higher work function compared to other reported nanoparticles, larger flexibility, and larger transparency [16,17]. In addition, the quantum confinement effect and the boundary effect of the GQDs make them a suitable charge-trapping medium [18]. For these reasons, in this study GQDs were used as a charge-trapping medium sandwiched between poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) layers to observe the resistive switching effect under the influence of an external voltage. The PMMA layer is a transparent thermoplastic that has been widely used to develop polymer-based electronic devices as a result of its excellent chemical, physical, biological, mechanical, optical and thermal properties [19–21].

Even though some investigations about multilevel resistive switching devices fabricated utilizing the graphene materials sandwiched between insulating polymer layers have been reported [2,9], the reported device fabrication process was not cost-efficient. Furthermore, when the top metal electrode contacts are formed via a thermal vacuum evaporation technique it requires long operating power consumption. Therefore, the comprehensive researches on multilevel resistive switching devices designed with multi-stacked GQD/PMMA layers by using a spray-coating method to form the top electrodes of the silver nanowires (Ag NWs) have been conducted in this work to replace the tedious thermal vacuum evaporation process. The devices with a MIM structure were fabricated on the top of indium-tin-oxide (ITO)-coated glass substrates by using the mentioned simple solution process. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) measurements were conducted to observe the cross-sectional structure of the fabricated devices. Current-voltage (I–V) measurements were performed to investigate the resistive switching behavior for the devices. The endurance cycle and the retention characteristics of the multi-resistance states for the devices were measured to investigate the stability of each resistance state. The conduction mechanisms of the Ag NW/PMMA/GQD/PMMA/GQD/PMMA/ITO/glass devices were described by using an energy-band diagram based on the I–V results.

The GQDs in deionized (DI) water (0.8 mg/ml) used in this study were supplied by the Ocean University of China [22]. A PMMA solution was prepared by dissolving 20 mg of PMMA in 1 ml of chlorobenzene, followed by 4 h of sonication before use. Two metal-insulator-metal (MIM) NVM devices with structures of (a) AgNW/PMMA/PMMA/PMMA/ITO/glass and (b) AgNW/PMMA/GQD/PMMA/GQD/PMMA/ITO/glass, denoted as MIM-A and MIM-B, respectively, were fabricated, as shown in Fig. 1 (a) and (b). The MIM-A device without GQDs in Fig. 1(a) is used as a reference sample in this study. Fig. 1(b) shows the MIM-B device with GQDs, and the inset in Fig. 1(b) represents a cross-sectional SEM image of the MIM-B NVM device with GQDs sandwiched between three PMMA stacking layers.

Prior to device fabrication, the ITO/glass substrate was ultrasonically cleaned in DI water, ethanol and DI water for 20 min each in that order. Then, the substrate was dried under a stream of nitrogen gas. The device fabrication began with a 30-nm-thick PMMA layer, which was deposited by spin-coating at 3000 rpm for 60 s and heated at 90 °C for 20 min. Before the GQDs were spin-coated at 2000 rpm for 60 s, a 5 W oxygen plasma treatment was performed for 5 s in order to reduce the hydrophobicity effect of the PMMA layer. Afterward, the subsequent PMMA and GQD layers were deposited using the same spin-coating speed for the same amount of time as mentioned above. Finally, AgNWs in isopropyle alcohol (IPA) were spray-coated at 0.1 MPa with the aid of a shadow mask and a 100 °C hot plate to form 0.5-mm-diameter circular top electrodes.

Current-voltage (I–V) measurements were performed on the memory devices to investigate the electrical properties of the NVM devices fabricated at room temperature by using a semiconductor characterization system (Keithley 4200-SCS), and bias voltages were applied to the top metal electrode with respect to the ITO bottom electrode for all measurements. A Shimadzu UV-VIS-NIR Spectrophotometer (UV-3600) was used to measure the transparency of the NVM devices. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Hitachi S-3000N) was used to obtain cross-sectional images of the devices.

Fig. 2 shows the transparencies of the MIM-A (reference) and the MIM-B NVM devices as functions of the wavelength in visible range from 400 to 800 nm. The absolute transmittances of the blank PET (black solid line) in a visible range vary from 86 to 91%. On the other hand, the absolute transmittances of the MIM-A (red solid line) and the MIM-B (blue solid line) devices, whose transmittances vary from 84–91%, and 82–90% in the visible spectrum, respectively, are slightly decreased in comparison with those of the blank ITO/glass structure. Thus, the obtained transparency results show that the transparencies of the reference sample, the MIM-A device without GQDs and the MIM-B device with embedded GQDs show negligible differences from the result for the blank ITO/glass structure.

Fig. 4 shows the energy-band diagrams used to describe the possible conduction mechanisms occurring in the MIM-B NVM device. The I-V curve in Fig. 3(a) was plotted on a double log scale in order to propose the conduction mechanisms in the MIM-B NVM device, and a curve fitting method was used in various double log I-V regions to identify the probable dominant conduction mechanisms. Fig. 4(a) shows the energy-band diagram when the NVM device stays in its initial state under a zero bias voltage, where LUMO and HOMO represent the energy levels of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital and the highest occupied molecular orbital, respectively. As in this study, the occurrence of thermionic emission current conduction at low voltages is a commonly-observed mechanism related to thermally-generated electrons [24,25]. In the low-voltage region, electron tunneling in the OFF state, as shown in Fig. 3(a), is unlikely to occur in the 30-nm-thick PMMA layer. Instead, the electrons might be transported over a metal–insulator interface via Schottky and Poole-Frenkel (PF) emissions. Therefore, the obtained experimental data in the OFF state region could be fitted well by using the relations ln (I/T2) α V0.5 and ln (I/V) α V0.5, respectively, where T is the temperature, indicating the presence of the Schottky and the PF emission mechanisms. When the voltage was applied to the AgNW electrode, a more plausible explanation is that the electrons from the AgNW electrode are transported across the PMMA barrier into the GQDs in a two-step process [23]: the electrons move into the PMMA layer due to thermionic emission via a field-assisted lowering of the barrier at the AgNW-PMMA interface; then, they move by PF emission based on the field-enhanced thermal emission of electrons trapped in the PMMA layer into the GQDs, as shown in Fig. 4(b). When the voltage is increased to V
ON1 , the curve-fitted slope is approximately 25, as shown in Fig. 3(a), and the transport mechanism switches to follow a typical space-charge-limited current model with traps, which is known as trapped-charge-limited current (TCLC) mechanism because the slope is much greater than 2 [26]. In this region, the first trap sites due to the presence of the GQDs start to be occupied by electrons, and an abrupt increase in current is observed when the additional excess charge appears in the PMMA layer [25], as shown in Fig. 4(b). Once all the first traps are filled through the TCLC mechanism, the device enters an intermediate ON-1 state, and additional excess charge is trapped in the middle PMMA layer, as shown in Fig. 4(c), before the onset of the next trapping of charge. The PF conduction mechanism is expected to be dominant at the intermediate ON-1 state in insulating thin films, as can be seen in Fig. 3(b), when the films have a large trap density and are thick enough to avoid quantum-mechanical tunneling [27,28]. The Coulomb potential energy of the electrons in this state can be reduced by applying a voltage higher than V
ON1 across the PMMA film [29]. The reduction in the potential energy might increase the probability of the electrons being thermally detrapped into a second set of GQD trap sites adjacent to the ITO electrode, as shown in Fig. 4(d) [29]. Again, when the voltage is increased to V
ON2 , the transport mechanism switches to follow the TCLC mechanism with a slope of about 34, and the second set of trap sites due to the presence of the GQDs starts to be occupied by electrons, resulting in an abrupt increase in current, as shown in Fig. 3(a). Once majority traps are filled through the TCLC mechanism, the device reaches a final ON state and is capable to generating a local internal field, and these trapped sites fill with carriers and form a conducting path in the film, suggesting the presence of Ohmic conduction with a slope of about 1, as seen in Fig. 3(c) [9]. The mismatch of the linear fit for the middle part of the ln(I) vs. ln(V) might originate from the slight fluctuation in the raw current-voltage data because the fluctuation is not obvious to be seen in semi-log plot, as shown in Fig. 3(c).

As discussed above, the NVM device is switched from the OFF state to the ON state via a multistage Set process. The device remains in an ON state even as the applied voltage is reduced to zero might be attributed to the trapping sites continuing to be occupied by electrons, indicative of the nonvolatility of the device. The device can be reset to an OFF state when the opposite polarity voltage is applied. The multistage Reset process observed in MIM-B, as shown in Fig. 3(a), might be associated with ruptures of conducting filaments with different values of V
OFF
[6,15,30]. When a negative voltage is applied to the top AgNW electrode, even though the current increases with increasing voltage due to the presence of charges trapped in the GQD trap sites, the current starts to decrease when the applied voltage reaches V
OFF1 because the de-trapping of electrons from the first set of GQD trap sites causes the conducting paths to break and brings the device to the OFF-1 state. Subsequently, the device returns to the OFF state at V
OFF2 when the de-trapping of electrons from the second set of GQD trap sites back to the AgNW electrode occurs, which is indicative of a disruption of the conducting filaments. This process was repeatable, and nonvolatile multistage Set-Reset switching could be obtained between the ON and the OFF states for a number of cycles by applying the proper bias voltage with the same forward and reverse-bias voltages.

Next, the endurance and the retention characteristics were examined to confirm the performance and the stability of the memory device, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . The endurance cycle in Fig. 5(a) was tested in the sequence of “Set I (1.3 V) – Read (0.35 V) – Set II (3 V) – Read (0.35 V) – Reset (−4 V) – Read (0.35 V)” with Set I, Set II, and Reset being the applied voltages to switch the memory device to the ON-1, ON, and OFF states, respectively. The three resistance states read at 0.35 V for over 1000 cycles remained stable with distinguishable values for each resistance state, as can be seen from Fig. 5(a). The stable resistance states are essential feature to avoid misreading of the three-value logic system with i.e. ON-1, ON, and OFF states for real applications. A retention test was performed, and the results are shown in Fig. 5(b). The same Set and Reset voltages were applied to the device, and the stabilities of the three resistance states for the devices were read at 0.35 V under ambient conditions for 1 × 104 s. The obtained retention results showed well-distinguished ON-1, ON, and OFF resistance states without any significant degradation over time.

Tristable switching NVM devices with a MIM structure based on GQDs embedded between multi-staked PMMA layers were fabricated by using a simple solution process. The I-V curves for the AgNW/PMMA/GQD/PMMA/GQD/PMMA/ITO/glass devices at 300 K showed a tristable switching with a large hysteresis window. The device's cycling endurance for the three resistance states remained stable with distinguishable values of three resistance states for over 1000 cycles. The obtained retention results showed well-distinguished resistance states without any significant degradation for up to 1 × 104 s. Schottky emission, PF emission, TCLC and ohmic conduction were proposed as the dominant conduction mechanisms for the fabricated NVM devices based on the obtained I-V characteristics. These results suggest that the fabricated devices utilizing multi-stacked GQDs sandwiched between PMMA layers hold promise for potential applications in next-generation high-density storage and three-value logic system.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Prof. Haibo Xu from the Ocean University of China for providing the graphene quantum dots. This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the gs1:National Research Foundation of Korea funded by the gs2:Ministry of Education, Science and Technology (2016R1A2A1A05005502) and by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61377027).
